segments.text: If red means danger, why do the signs that saves life glow green? Red is the color we see everywhere for danger. Fire
trucks, alarms, and stop button. But when it is a time to escape, the signs above us glow green. Why is that? In
1985, British Air Tours Flight 28M caught fire on the runway. Smoke filled the cabin and the passengers crumbled to
find the exit. Many were close to the safety but could not see the red exit signs. The red color just disappeared in the thick smoke. After this tragedy,
scientists studied how people actually see colors in the emergencies. They found out green shows up much better than red in dark smokeoky conditions.
Red look bright in daylight, but it fades when it's needed the most. Green stays visible and guides people to safety. Because of this, safety rules
changed worldwide. Exit signs switched from red to green on airplanes,
airports, buildings. New symbols and arrows were added to help everyone escape no matter their language or fear.
That soft green light isn't our design choice. It's a result of hard lessons,
care, and smart thinking. It reminds us that the good design is not only the one who is shouting the loudest. It's about helping people when it counts. If you
found this interesting, subscribe for more such amazing design stories.
